eraMap.title: The 323i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: First-generation 323i — SOHC 2.3L six; K-Jetronic injection; classic BMW six-cylinder character
Revised 731 head with larger ports; L-Jetronic fuel injection; 139–150 hp
DOHC 2.5L six — aluminium block, single VANOS; smooth, reliable, but cooling system is the weak point
Dual VANOS update to M52; 2.5L six; 170 hp; cooling system and DISA issues are the main concerns
Magnesium/aluminium composite block; Valvetronic; 177–190 hp; oil leaks and eccentric shaft sensor are common

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current 323i replacement costs across all generations. The M52 and N52 variants typically sit at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 323i engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 323i? Check These First
buyIf: * Cooling system recently overhauled (E36/E46 — water pump, thermostat, radiator)
* No oil leaks (or recent gasket replacements — valve cover, oil filter housing)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (all generations)
* For E46: DISA valve functioning correctly (no rattles or loss of torque)
* For E90: N52 with documented oil filter housing gasket replacement
avoidIf: * Overheating history or unexplained coolant loss — head gasket may be compromised
* Visible oil leaks around the oil filter housing (E90 — belt damage risk)
* Rattling from the intake manifold (E46 — DISA valve may be failing)
* Poor service history — all 323i engines are sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 323i is one of BMW's most enduring badges — spanning five generations from the E21 to the E90. The engines are fundamentally robust, but each era has its own character and weaknesses: the M20 is a classic SOHC six with great character; the M52 and M52TU are smooth, reliable DOHC units with cooling system vulnerabilities; the N52 is a modern masterpiece with oil leak tendencies. Budget for cooling system maintenance on the E36/E46 and oil gasket replacements on the E90. With typical values ranging from £3,000 (E36) to £8,000+ (E90), an engine replacement is borderline on older examples but worthwhile on cherished cars.

cta.label: → Compare 323i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 3 Series 323i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks, recent water pump/thermostat replacement (E36/E46 critical) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for smooth idle; any rattles from the intake (DISA valve) or timing chain area?
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover, oil filter housing, and oil pan for leaks
* Service History — Including which engine era (M20, M52, M52TU or N52) and oil-change discipline
* VANOS Operation — Test low-end torque; any flat spot or hesitation may indicate VANOS seal wear
* Temperature Gauge — Should sit at 12 o'clock; any movement above is a red flag
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate VANOS, DISA or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (all are petrol)

items.question: How much does a BMW 3 Series 323i engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned 323i engine typically costs £2,500–£5,500 supply only, depending on the generation. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £800–£1,500, but condition varies dramatically. Reconditioned E46 323i engines are available from approximately £1,595.

items.question: Is the BMW 3 Series 323i engine reliable?
items.answer: The 323i badge spans five generations, each with different engine families — but all are fundamentally robust six-cylinder petrol engines. The M20 (E21/E30) is a classic SOHC design with great longevity. The M52/M52TU (E36/E46) are smooth, reliable DOHC units — the cooling system is their main weakness. The N52 (E90) is a modern magnesium/aluminium engine with excellent reliability — oil leaks are the main concern.

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series 323i?
items.answer: Cooling system failures on the M52 and M52TU (water pump, thermostat housing, radiator), oil leaks across all generations (particularly valve cover and oil filter housing on the N52), DISA valve failure on the M52TU (E46), and VANOS seal wear on the M52/M52TU. None are catastrophic if caught early.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a 323i engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the generation and vehicle condition. Against a well-kept E46 worth £4,000–£8,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 50–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired E36 worth £2,000–£4,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. E90 values (£5,000–£10,000) make replacement more viable.

items.question: How do I find my BMW 3 Series 323i engine code?
Can I repair the M52 cooling system, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: The cooling system components must be replaced — the water pump, thermostat housing, and radiator are all known failure points on the M52 and M52TU. A preventative overhaul at 60,000–80,000 miles costs £300–£800. Ignoring it can lead to sudden overheating and head gasket failure, which is far more expensive.
